coredata.dc:title: Luminescent composite organohydrogels with Fe3+, pH, and glucose-dependent shape memory behavior accompanied with diverse fluorescence variation
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yanqin
Zhu, Yu
Zhang, Xuehui
Zhu, Fengbo
Qin, Yi-Xian
Chen, Weiyi
Zheng, Qiang
coredata.dc:description: It is quite urgent to develop a smart and high through-put confidential information storage, protection, and display platform based on the fluorescent hydrogels that could exhibit diverse fluorescent variation behavior during each stimulus-based shape memory and recovery process. Herein, it proposed an effective approach to develop a novel fluorescent composite gel with Fe3+, pH, and glucose-dependent diversity fluorescence variation and shape memory behavior. For this composite gel system, based on the reversible coordination interactions between Fe3+ and carboxylic acid groups of carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), boric acid groups of carbon dots (CDs), and hydroxyl groups of PVA, it can stabilize and memorize the temporary shapes under the stimulus of Fe3+ and pH, respectively. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of the CDs within the gels can also exhibit diversity variation behaviors during each shape recovery process. Furthermore, the compositions of the composite gel were optimized to realize relatively high efficiency of shape recovery and fluorescence variation under each type of stimulus. In summary, this study presented a universal strategy for multi-shape memory hydrogels with diverse fluorescence variation properties which exhibited prominent prospective in the field of information encryption with high-level security.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Shape memory/recovery
Multi-stimulus responsive
Fluorescence visualization
Organohydrogels
Multi-network

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the luminescent composite organohydrogel
Shape memory properties of the luminescent composite organohydrogel
Design of the luminescent composite organohydrogel
Multi-stimulus dependent fluorescence variation behavior of the luminescent composite organohydrogel
Mechanical properties of the luminescent composite organohydrogel
Antibacterial performance of the luminescent composite organohydrogel
Multi-stimulus responsive shape memory properties of the luminescent composite organohydrogel and its mechanism
Self-recovery and fatigue resistance capacity of the luminescent composite organohydrogel

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of the composite hydrogels: a) CMC/PVA hydrogel; b) CMC/PVA organohydrogel (CP-OH, the volume ratios of DMSO/H2O: 4:2); and c) CDs/CMC/PVA organohydrogel (CCP-OH, the volume ratios of DMSO/H2O: 4:2; the mass ratios of CDs/PVA: 1:8).

Fluorescence spectra of the CDs/CMC/PVA organohydrogel at a) different Fe3+ concentrations of 10-5, 10-4, 10-3, 10-2, 10-1 mol/L; b) different pH values of 4, 6, 8, 10, 12; and c) different glucose concentrations of 10-5, 10-4, 10-3, 10-2, 10-1 mol/L. (λex = 410 nm).

a) Tensile stress–strain curves and b) Corresponding mechanical parameters of the CMC/PVA organohydrogel which fabricated with different volume ratios of DMSO/H2O (0:6, 1:5, 2:4, 3:3, 4:2, 5:1, 6:0); c) Tensile stress–strain curves and d) Corresponding mechanical parameters of the CDs/CMC/PVA organohydrogel which prepared with different mass ratios of CDs/PVA (0:8, 1:8, 2:8, 3:8, 4:8), e) Freezing tolerance behaviors of the CDs/CMC/PVA organohydrogels by compressing with same pressure (Before tasting, the gel specimens were kept at −25℃ for 24 h).

Antibacterial activity assay (G1: pure agar plates; G2: CMC/PVA hydrogel; G3: CMC/PVA organohydrogel; G4 group: CDs/CMC/PVA organohydrogel): (a) representative unit images of S. aureus; (b) representative unit images of E. coli.

a) Photographs demonstrating the transition process for the CDs/CMC/PVA organohydrogel from the temporary shape to the original shape in the i) EDTA solution (0.3 mol/L), ii) PBS solution (pH = 4), and iii) glucose solution (0.1 mol/L) under UV irradiation; b) The shape fixity ratios and shape recovery ratios under three different stimulation of Fe3+, pH and glucose, respectively; c) Schematic illustration of the responsive mechanism for the CDs/CMC/PVA organohydrogel which exhibiting diversity fluorescence variation and shape memory behavior under the stimulus of i) Fe3+, ii) pH, and iii) glucose, respectively.

Eminent self-recovery and fatigue resistance capacity of the CDs/CMC/PVA organohydrogel. a) Self-recovery behaviors, b) recovery efficiency as a function of resting time upon stretching deformation; c) Consecutive 10 uniaxial stretching-relaxation cycles at a strain of 100%, d) Hysteresis curves of multiple samples with various strains; e) Uniaxial stretching-relaxation cycles of one sample with different strains; f) Consecutive 100 uniaxial stretching-relaxation cycles at a strain of 50%.

a) Schematic diagram of the design principle and molecular chains architecture for the luminescent composite; b) a proof-of-concept material system attempting to demonstrate the shape designing and fluorescence information encoding for dual-encryption.

head.ce:title: Luminescent composite organohydrogels with Fe3+, pH, and glucose-dependent shape memory behavior accompanied with diverse fluorescence variation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Biomedical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ministry of Education Key Laboratory of Macromolecular Synthesis and Functionalization
Department of Polymer Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ministry of Education Key Laboratory of Macromolecular Synthesis and Functionalization, Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310027, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Biomedical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China (YQ. Wang) College of Materials Science & Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China (Q. Zheng).

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to the manuscript.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is quite urgent to develop a smart and high through-put confidential information storage, protection, and display platform based on the fluorescent hydrogels that could exhibit diverse fluorescent variation behavior during each stimulus-based shape memory and recovery process. Herein, it proposed an effective approach to develop a novel fluorescent composite gel with Fe3+, pH, and glucose-dependent diversity fluorescence variation and shape memory behavior. For this composite gel system, based on the reversible coordination interactions between Fe3+ and carboxylic acid groups of carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), boric acid groups of carbon dots (CDs), and hydroxyl groups of PVA, it can stabilize and memorize the temporary shapes under the stimulus of Fe3+ and pH, respectively. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of the CDs within the gels can also exhibit diversity variation behaviors during each shape recovery process. Furthermore, the compositions of the composite gel were optimized to realize relatively high efficiency of shape recovery and fluorescence variation under each type of stimulus. In summary, this study presented a universal strategy for multi-shape memory hydrogels with diverse fluorescence variation properties which exhibited prominent prospective in the field of information encryption with high-level security.

ce:section.ce:para: SMGs are considered one of the smart materials[1–4] that can fix temporary shapes and recover to their original shape under various kinds of external stimuli, such as pH[5,6], thermal[7], light[8], or specific chemical reagents[9,10]. Due to their prominent stimuli-based shape memory/recovery abilities, they have shown promising application in many fields, such as biomedical engineering, anti-counterfeiting, and information encryption, et al [11]. Particularly, in the information security fields, to develop a smart confidential information storage, protection, and display platform for safer data storage and protection[12,13], it’s brilliant to develop shape memory gels (SMGs) which could achieve multi-level encrypted with the shape-morphing ability and patterned fluorescence color by the control of various external stimuli.

Compared with conventional SMGs, SMGs entrapped with fluorophore are attractive for information security applications owing to their advantages of convenient operation, and facile in exploring the dynamic process of shape or conformation changes visually[14,15]. To date, it has developed numerous SMGs with excellent shape and fluorescence change behaviors under single-factor stimulation which has been applied in information encryption. For example, Wang’s group [16] prepared a composite gel in which the change of the shape and fluorescence occurred simultaneously under the single stimulus of pH; Tang’s group [17] reported a smart hydrogel with shape memory property and tunable fluorescence behavior under the stimulation of Fe3+. The hydrogel achieved shape memory behavior based on the coordination between Fe3+ and the carboxylic acid groups, and at the same time, its fluorescence could also be quenched by Fe3+. However, for multi-stimuli responsive SMGs with fluorescence, they generally responded to various stimuli based on two or more different kinds of reversible chemical bonds. Thus, when the SMGs were exposed to different types of stimuli, it was difficult to ensure the fluorescence signals of the fixed fluorophores within the polymer matrix exhibited diverse variations without interference. For example, in Zhou’s work [15], they have prepared an unprecedented stimuli-responsive fluorescence SMG based on the reversible dissociation and reconstruction of the metal–ligand coordination and borate ester bonds when exposed to acid/base, salt, and redox stimuli. However, the three different stimuli-based reversible shape memories process just accompanied by the same fluorescence signal variation behavior (fluorescence intensity changed from strong to weak). To construct SMGs with diverse fluorescence variation behavior during the different stimuli-based shape recovery process, so that to realize a relatively high through-put confidential information encryption, it was brilliant to ensure each type of dynamic reversible chemical bond which formed between the fluorophore and polymers could independently respond to a specific stimulus. A more reliable condition was that the dissociation and reconstruction of each reversible interaction between the fluorophore and polymers should also based on different mechanisms, such as the transformation of molecular structures [18], and electron transfer [19,20] or intermolecular aggregations [21], et al.

Herein, it developed a potential information encryption platform based on a novel luminescent composite gel with Fe3+, pH, and glucose-dependent shape memory behavior accompanied by diverse fluorescence variation. Briefly, the temporary shapes of the composite hydrogels could be stabilized and memorized by the triggering of the Fe3+, pH, and glucose, respectively. Meanwhile, during the dissociation and reconstruction of the reversible non-covalent bonds between the fluorophore and polymer matrix, the fluorescence signals (emission wavelength or intensity) of the carbon dots (CDs) within the hydrogel system also experienced different variations based on different mechanisms, such as electron transfer and intermolecular aggregation. In addition, the DMSO/water two-phase solvents system endowed the luminescent composite gels with strong freezing tolerance abilities, so that the composite hydrogels could work smoothly at −25℃ with deformability and outstanding durability. Moreover, the composite gel possessed excellent antibacterial activity owing to the bacteriostasis of DMSO. This work should open opportunities for gel materials in the field of high through-put information encryption and display with high-level security.

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, degree of alcoholysis of 98%-99%), carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC, DS = 0.7, M.W 90000), glutaraldehyde (50%), 4-carboxyphenyl boronic acid (CPBA, 97%), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, ≥99.8%) were analytical grade and received from Aladdin Chemistry Co (Shanghai, China). FeCl3·6H2O (99%) and ethylene imine polymer (BPEI, M.W. 1800, 99%) were obtained from Macklin Chemical Co (Shanghai, China). HCl were analytical grade reagents from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Citrate acid (CA, 99%) was purchased from Jiuding Chemical Co (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the luminescent composite organohydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical preparation procedure, 2 g PVA was dissolved in a 12.0 mL mixed solution of deionized water and DMSO with different volume ratios (0:6, 1:5, 2:4, 3:3, 4:2, 5:1, 6:0). Subsequently, a certain amount of CMC solution (5 wt%) and CDs powder (0, 0.25 g, 0.50 g, 0.75 g, 1.00 g) was added and mixed for a further 30 min in room temperature, followed by slow addition of 1.0 M HCl to adjust the pH of the mixed solution to be about 3.0. Next, 0.44 mL glutaraldehyde with a concentration of 5.0% was added to the solution and stirred rapidly. After that, the transparent solution was transferred into polypropylene molds and kept in a water bath at 50 °C for 30 min. For the sake of brevity, the CMC/PVA hydrogel, CMC/PVA organohydrogel, and CDs/CMC/PVA organohydrogel were denoted as CP-H, CP-OHy, and CxCP-OHy, respectively. Specifically, x referred to the mass ratio of CDs/PVA, and y referred to the volume ratio of DMSO/H2O. The specific compositions of all the samples were shown in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Shape memory properties of the luminescent composite organohydrogel
ce:section.ce:para:
For evaluation of the shape memory performance, the CCP-OHs gels were cut into strips with the size of 30.0 mm × 5.0 mm × 2.0 mm. The shape memory properties were tested as follows:
1)
Fe3+-based stimuli-responsive test: the straight strips of the CCP-OHs gels were firstly bent to an angle (360°) by an external force. Then the deformed specimens were immersed in Fe3+ solution to fix the temporary shape. The Fe3+-based shape recovery process was investigated by immersing the specimens in a 0.3 M EDTA solution.
2)
pH-based stimuli-responsive test: the straight strips of the CCP-OH gels were bent to an angle (360°) by an external force, and then they were immersed into alkaline buffer solution (pH = 10) to fix the temporary shape. Then, the pH-based shape recovery process was investigated by immersing the specimens in an acid buffer solution (pH = 4.0).
3)
Glucose-based stimuli-responsive test: the straight strips of the CCP-OH gels were bent to an angle (360°) by an external force, and then they were immersed in alkaline buffer solution (pH = 10) to fix the temporary shape. The glucose-based shape recovery process was investigated by immersing the specimens in a 0.1 M glucose solution.

It should be noted that the shape memory and recovery process were recorded under daylight and UV light, respectively. The shape fixity ratio (Rf) and shape recovery ratio (Rr) were calculated by the following equations of (1) and (2):
R f
=
θ f
θ g
× 100 %
R r
=
θ f
-
θ t
θ f
× 100 %
where θg was the given angle, θf was the fixed angle, and θt was the recovered angle at the time of t.

Mechanical properties of the composite organohydrogels were tested using a Universal Testing Machine (Instron 3343, USA). For the tensile test, the samples were cut into a dumbbell shape with a length of 30 mm, width of 4 mm, and thickness of 2 mm. And they were measured at a rate of 30 mm·min−1 until breaking occurred at room temperature. Samples with a cylinder shape (height of 10 mm and diameter of 10 mm) were prepared for the compression tests (compression rate: 10% of original height/min, maximum compressive deformation: 80%). The nominal stress–strain curves were recorded, and the elastic modulus was calculated from the slope of the initial linear region (5 ∼ 15% strain) of the stress−strain curves. The toughness of the gels was characterized by the integral area of the stress–strain curves.

Cyclic uniaxial stretching-relaxation at different stretching strains or different resting times was also measured at room temperature to characterize the anti-fatigue and self-recovery properties. The velocity was fixed at 20 mm·min−1. For each sample, a minimum of three measurements was taken, and the average value was reported.

The fluorescence properties of the original composite organohydrogels were measured by a Hitachi F-2700 spectrofluorometer at room temperature. Emission spectra of the gels were collected at different excitation wavelengths with emission and excitation slit of 10 nm. Organohydrogel samples cultured with Fe3+ (10-5 to 10-1 M), PBS solution (pH = 2.0 ∼ 12.0), and glucose (10-5 to 10-1 M) were obtained and measured, respectively. For each measurement, the samples were placed in the solution for 30 min and then washed with deionized water for 5 min before testing to remove the remaining substances on the surface of the gel.

E. coli (Gram-negative bacteria) and S. aureus (Gram-positive bacteria) were used as typical bacteria to evaluate the antibacterial property of the organohydrogels. Wafer samples with a diameter of 10 mm were used for antibacterial assessments, and all samples were sterilized by UV for 1 h. Both S. aureus and E. coli bacteria were inoculated over the surface of the sterile agar plates. After the plates containing the bacteria dried, the samples gently adhered to the agar. The plates containing the samples were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Finally, the plates were photographed and the bacteriostatic zone was measured to investigate the antibacterial effect of the composite organohydrogels.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed at room temperature by a Burke alpha II spectrometer (Karlsruhe, Germany). Set the resolution to be 4 cm−1 and the scanning range to be 4000 ∼ 400 cm−1.

The section microstructures of the freeze-dried organohydrogels were observed by the scanning electron microscope (JSM-7100F, JEOL). The organohydrogels were freeze-dried with a freeze drier (FD-1A-80, China) for 24 h.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of the organohydrogels with different volume ratios of DMSO to H2O was carried out on an X-ray diffractometer (Haoyuan 2700BH, China) from 2θ angle of 10° to 40° at a scanning speed of 10° min−1.

The swelling ratio and water content of different organohydrogels were measured using a gravimetric method at room temperature as follows. The samples (column) were freeze-dried until constant weight. The swelling ratio at a different time and water content of each organohydrogels were calculated using the following equations:
(3)
swelling
r a t i o
=
W t
- -
W 0
/
W 0
×
100 %
(4)
water
c o n t e n t
=
W 1
- -
W 0
/
W 1
×
100 %
where W0 was the weight of the dry samples, Wt was the weight of the sample after absorption of water for t min, and W1 was the weight of the hydrogels before drying, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the luminescent composite organohydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication principle for the luminescent composite organohydrogel with the ability of high through-put information encryption was illustrated in Scheme1 a. Firstly, PVA, CMC, and CDs were mixed homogeneously in DMSO/H2O binary solvent systems. Then, PVA chains were covalently crosslinked with glutaraldehyde at pH = 3 for the primary polymer network. At the same time, the CMC chains were evenly embedded within the polymer network and fixed by hydrogen bonds between –OH groups of CMC and PVA. To endow the composite gel with luminescent properties, the as-prepared CDs with abundant functional groups of -B-O–H, –COOH, and –NH2 were also entrapped within the hydrogel system. In the aqueous environment of strong acid, during the crosslinking process of PVA by the GA, the –NH2 on the CDs would also act with the –CHO of the GA, so that the CDs could also be linked to the PVA network by the chemical bonds. The particles of the CDs were mostly spherical in shape and typical amorphous carbonaceous structure with discernible lattices (as shown in the HRTEM image of Fig. S1). To track the microstructure evolution process for the composite organohydrogel, it has investigated the SEM images of the CMC/PVA hydrogel (CP-H gel), CMC/PVA organohydrogel (CP-OH gel), and CDs/CMC/PVA organohydrogel (CCP-OH gel). As shown in Fig. 1 a, the surface of the CP-H gel was smooth and without an obvious mesh structure. Compared with the CP-H gel, the CP-OH gel showed a winkled structure with the appearance of obvious pores (Fig. 1b). It is probably due to the addition of DMSO that resulted in the complexity of the network skeleton. For the CCP-OH gel (Fig. 1c), with the appearance of fine CDs particles, its surface became more wrinkle and denser but the pore structures disappeared. In Fig. S2, it showed the FTIR spectrum of the dried CP-H (curve a), CP-OH (curve b), and CCP-OH (curve c) gels, respectively. For the CCP-OH gels (curve c), the characteristic absorption peak around 3382 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibrations bonds of –OH groups on the PVA [22]. The stretching vibration peak of the -B-OH group which originated from the embedded CDs shifted from 1432 cm−1 (as shown in curve c) to 1462 cm−1 (as shown in curve d). It was owing to the formation of the intra- and inter-chain hydrogen bonds between the CDs and PVA chains [23–25]. In addition, for the CP-OH (curve b) and CCP-OH (curve c) gels, with the appearance of DMSO, it appeared the characteristic peaks at 938 and 1023 cm−1, which correspond to the S-C and S=O stretching vibration bands, respectively [26,27].

In Scheme 1b, it exhibited a proof-of-concept material system attempting to demonstrate the shape designing and fluorescence information encoding for dual-encryption. For this composite organohydrogel, in the information encryption stage, based on the reversible coordination interactions between Fe3+ and carboxylic acid groups of carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), boric acid groups of carbon dots (CDs) and hydroxyl groups of PVA, it can stabilize and memorize the temporary shapes under the stimulus of Fe3+ and alkali, respectively. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of the CDs within the gels can also exhibit diversity variation behaviors, such as fluorescence quenching behavior based on the Fe3+-stimuli and fluorescence emission color variation behavior based on the alkali-stimuli; While in the information decryption stage, with the appearance of EDTA, acid and glucose, respectively, accompanied with the shape recovery process, the fluorescence of the composite gel could also exhibit different variation behaviors. More importantly, the encryption and decryption processes were even recyclable. Thus, a dual-encryption information storage and display platform with shape designing and fluorescence information encoding abilities has been developed brilliantly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-stimulus dependent fluorescence variation behavior of the luminescent composite organohydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs with abundant functional groups (such as the carboxyl groups, the borate groups, and the amino groups) were incorporated into the organohydrogels for the CCP-OH gels with Fe3+, pH, and glucose-dependent fluorescence variation behavior. As shown in Fig. 2 , it showed the fluorescence spectra of the CCP-OH gels under the stimuli of Fe3+, pH, and glucose, respectively. For the Fe3+-based responsive behavior, originally, the CCP-OH gels showed strong fluorescence emission at 475 nm, while the fluorescence intensity of the gels decreased with the increase of the concentration of Fe3+ (Fig. 2a). The intensity change was distinct enough for naked-eye observation. As illustrated in the images in Fig. 2a, the fluorescence of the gel changed from bright green to colorless obviously, when the concentrations of Fe3+ increased from 10-5 to 10-1 mol/L. This may be attributed to the fluorescence quenching of the CDs by Fe3+ based on the electron transferring mechanism[28,29]. Fig. 2b illustrated the fluorescence variation of the CCP-OH gels in the environment with the pH ranging from 4.0 to 12.0. In the strong acid condition (pH = 4.0), the fluorescence emission wavelength was at 487 nm, while it moved to a shorter wavelength of 471 nm in the strong basic condition (pH = 12.0), which implied that the fluorescence emission wavelength of the as-prepared CCP-OH gels was very sensitive to the pH of the environment. As shown in the illustration of Fig. 2b, the obtained gels undergo a clear and instant color change from bright green to blue. The fluorescence switchable property of the CCP-OH gels can be ascribed to the protonation and deprotonation of the carboxyl groups on the CD’s surface in acid or basic solutions, respectively[30,31]. Specifically, under acid conditions, the carboxyl groups were protonated, which formed some non-radiative recombination centers on the surface of the CDs and reduced their fluorescence intensity. However, in the basic condition, these carboxyl groups of the CDs were deprotonated and their fluorescence and wavelength were restored. In addition, when the composite organohydrogel was immersed in the glucose solution, it was clear that the fluorescence of the CCP-OH gel was relatively stable under different concentrations of glucose (Fig. 2c). Either the fluorescence emission wavelength or the fluorescence emission intensity would keep its original state without the bother of the glucose (as shown in the images of Fig. 2c). This may be attributed to the fact that the fluorescence of the CDs would not be interfered with by the glucose[32,33]. In summary, the distinctive fluorescence variation performance of the CCP-OH gel under the stimuli of Fe3+, pH, and glucose were particularly attractive for naked-eye sensing, thus, it was prospective to be applied in the designing of the intelligent visual SMGs systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of the luminescent composite organohydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanical properties were essential factors for the SMGs experiencing shape deformation and recovery. With the addition of the different components, it affected not only the composition and structure of the gel, but also the mechanical properties and deformation abilities. To evaluate the contribution of DMSO and CDs to the mechanical properties of the organohydrogel, the samples with different volume ratios of DMSO/H2O and mass ratios of CDs/PVA were measured by tensile and compressive tests, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 a and 3b, with the volume ratio of the DMSO/H2O ranging from 0:6 to 5:1, the tensile strength and breaking strain of the CP-OH gels increased from 0.08 MPa, 229.8% to 1.7 MPa, 503.1%, respectively. When the volume ratio of DMSO/H2O continued to increase to 6:0, the tensile strength and breaking strain of the gel decreased slightly to 1.6 MPa, 488.2% respectively, which was higher than that of pure CP-H gel. Similarly, for the CP-OH gel, its maximum Young's modulus enhanced to be 0.5 MPa which was 13.6 times higher than the pure CP-H gel. The toughness of the CP-OH gel reached to be 2327 kJ/m3, which was 45.9 times higher than the pure CP-H gel. Especially, with the increased volume ratio of the DMSO/H2O from 0:6 to 5:1, Young's modulus of the CP-OH gel increased from 0.04 MPa to 0.16 MPa (as shown in Fig. 3b). Typical compressive stress–strain curves of the CP-OH gels with different volume ratios of DMSO/H2O were also shown in Fig. S3a and S3b. For the compressive toughness, it increased from 15.2 kJ·m−3 to 206.8 kJ·m−3. The enhanced compression mechanical performance was due to the synergistic effect of the DMSO/H2O binary solvent system. As reported in Ye’s work [34], when the volume ratio of the DMSO/H2O in their hydrogel system reached up to 6:4, a network structure based on 1DMSO/2H2O would be formed. The crystallization of the PVA was restricted by such a structure and presented a growth trend along with the two-dimensional directions [27,35]. A similar phenomenon could also be seen in our composite organohydrogel system, which could be further confirmed by the XRD characterization (as shown in Fig. S4). The XRD patterns of CP-OH showed prominent peaks around 2θ = 20°, which coincided with the diffraction peaks of the standard PVA pattern. With the increase in the volume ratio of DMSO/H2O, the intensity of the diffraction peaks showed a decreasing trend. However, as shown in Fig. 3b, when the volume ratio of the DMSO/H2O reached up to 6:0, it would lead to a slight decrease in the mechanical properties. It was mainly due to that excessive DMSO molecules might affect or even destroy the complete structure, so that the DMSO/H2O network structure disappeared. Under comprehensive consideration, the optimized volume ratio of the DMSO/H2O should be kept as 4:2 in the following experiments. The mechanical properties of the composite organohydrogel with different mass ratios of CDs/PVA were also evaluated to obtain the optimized composition ratios for the CCP-OH gels. As shown in the Fig. 3c and 3d, as the mass ratio of the CDs/PVA increased from 0:8 to 4:8, the tensile strength, Young's modulus, and toughness of the CCP-OH gel decreased from 1.4 MPa, 0.4 MPa, and 1427.4 kJ·m−3 to 0.3 MPa, 0.04 MPa, and 201.3 kJ·m−3, respectively. However, the tensile breaking strain increased from 404.7% to 438.6%. As for the compressive properties, the compressive strength (at the compression strain of 80%), elasticity modulus, and toughness of the CCP-OH gel decreased from 1.4 MPa, 0.2 MPa, and 250.9 kJ·m−3 to 0.2 MPa, 0.08 MPa, and 16.9 kJ·m−3, respectively (Figs. S3c and S3d). This could be attributed as follows. The existence of CDs increased the local polymer density and chain entanglement, which further affected the uniformity of the gel network. The enhanced entanglement of the chains resulted in a decrease in the stretchability of the hydrogel. In addition, as we all know, DMSO was widely used for cryopreserving cells, and had been recently used for fabricating anti-freezing hydrogel. As shown in Fig. 3e, when frozen at −25 ℃ for 24 h, the CP-H gel can easily break into fragments by hand due to the formation of ice crystals within the hydrogel. In contrast, the CP-OH and CCP-OH gels can withstand cyclic twisting, bending, and folding, even under −25 ℃. Previous studies [34] have shown similar results; and such freezing tolerance behavior can be ascribed to the DMSO/H2O binary solvent. DMSO molecules can form hydrogen bonds with water molecules with the molar ratio of 1:2; thus a stable six-ring configuration could restrain the formation of ice crystals at sub-zero temperature and endow anti-freezing performance for the as-developed organohydrogels [36,37].

Furthermore, the water content, swelling ratios, and water loss ratios of the composite organohydrogels with different compositions were also characterized. As shown in Fig. S5, the water content showed a prominent decrease from 77.6% to 69.8% due to the addition of DMSO. And the swelling ratios of all the gels increased quickly within the initial 50 min. When reached equilibrium, the swelling ratios remained stable at nearly 200.7%, 116.1%, and 183.4% for CP-H, CP-OH, and CCP-OH, respectively. (Fig. S5b). It is probably due to the formation of the DMSO/H2O six-ring configuration that resulted in the complexity of the gel network skeleton. What need to be pointed out was that all gels could maintain a stable shape and weight after the swelling test. In addition, the water loss ratio of the gels was also investigated, as shown in Fig. S5c. The CP-H gels reached a steady state within 100 min compared with 350 min of the CP-OH and CCP-OH gels. It was due to the addition of the organic solvents of DMSO that endowed the gel with long-term water retention and prolonged its service life of the gel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial performance of the luminescent composite organohydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: It was of significance to endow the shape memory gels with antibacterial properties so that they could be safely applied as stimulus-responsive actuators in the biomedical engineering fields. The composite gel of CMC/PVA hydrogel (CP-H gel), CMC/PVA organohydrogel (CP-OH gel), and CDs/CMC/PVA organohydrogel (CCP-OH gel) against two typical pathogenic bacteria (Gram-negative E. coli and Gram-positive S. aureus) were evaluated as shown in Fig. 4 . The inhibition zone test was applied to analyze the antibacterial behavior of the control (G1), CP-H (G2), CP-OH (G3), CCP-OH (G4) gel. After cultivation for 24 h in a 37 ℃ incubator, there was no antibacterial ring on the culture dish for the G1 group and G2 group. Compared with the CP-H gel, the distance between the CP-OH gel and the bacteriostasis circle was about 0.66 cm for S. aureus and 0.82 cm for E. coli, implying the CP-OH gel maintained more prominent bacterial inhibition performances. And G4 showed nearly the same phenomenon as the G3, the bacteriostatic zone radius of the CCP-OH gel was 0.67 cm for S. aureus and 0.90 cm for E. coli, respectively, indicating that the growth of both S. aureus and E. coli was efficiently inhibited. The proposed antimicrobial mechanism of DMSO is mainly based on the variation of the bacterial cell membrane [38]: (1) it can cause the conformation changes for the cell membrane proteins; (2) change the cell membrane surface protein channel activity; (3) reduce the bacterial cell membrane potential. In summary, the composite hydrogels have an excellent antibacterial effect against Gram-positive bacteria and Gram-negative bacteria. The antibacterial activity of as-developed shape memory hydrogels would expand its potential application in the biomedical engineering field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-stimulus responsive shape memory properties of the luminescent composite organohydrogel and its mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To characterize the shape memory properties of the CDs/CMC/PVA organohydrogels under different stimuli of Fe3+, pH, and glucose solution, respectively, the gel samples were first bent into circles by an external force, followed by fixing the temporary shape under different treatments. Briefly, (Ι) As shown in Fig. 5 a (i) and Movie S1, after being treated with the Fe3+ solution (0.1 M), the temporary shape of the CCP-OH gels was well fixed based on the coordination of the Fe3+–COO-. And during this process, it accompanied the fluorescence quenching of the gels. However, after being immersed in EDTA solution (0.3 M), Fe3+ could be extracted by EDTA, the gel recovered to its steady-state shape slowly within 11 min and its fluorescence recovered to the original color of green (Fig. 5c (i)); (Ⅱ) As shown in Fig. 5a (ii) and Movie S2, when the CCP-OH gel with green fluorescence was immersed in Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 10), its temporary shape can be fixed by the formation of boric acid ester bonds. Nevertheless, when the gels were treated with PBS buffer solution (pH = 4), the gel recovered to its steady-state shape within 5 min. Due to the variation of pH, the groups on the surface of the CDs underwent protonation to deprotonation. As a result, the fluorescence emission color of the sample also varied gradually from green to blue (Fig. 5c (ii)); (Ш) once the twisty CCP-OH gel, fixed in Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 10), was immersed into glucose solution (0.1 M), the gel recovered slowly to its original shape within 7 min without any fluorescence color or intensity change (Fig. 5a (iii) and Movie S3). The fixed shape recovered due to the glucose competitively bonded boric acid bonds as shown in Fig. 5c (iii). It was noteworthy that the pH-switching and Fe3+-switching behavior displayed good reversibility.

To acquire more information about the shape fixity ratio (Rf) and shape recovery ratio (Rr) of the composite organohydrogel under different stimuli of Fe3+, pH, and glucose, respectively, more detailed investigations were shown in Fig. 5b. The Rr and Rr of the gels were obtained by bending experiments performed on O-shaped samples. As shown in Fig. 5b, when the original gel samples were immersed into Fe3+ solution (0.1 M) for the fixing of its temporary shape, the Rf can reach up to 97%. And the Rr can reach 88% when 0.3 M EDTA was applied to induce the shape recovery. Similarly, it could be seen that when the original gel samples were immersed in Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 10), the Rf could reach to be about 95%, and a higher Rr can be observed when it was immersed in PBS buffer solution (pH = 4) with a maximum Rr of 87%. The Rf reached to be about 95% after being immersed in Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 10), and when it was immersed in glucose solution (0.1 mol·L-1), a relatively lower Rr value of 73% was presented. These results demonstrated that all the reversible cross-links could be employed to keep the temporary shapes of the organohydrogels, and achieve shape-memory performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-recovery and fatigue resistance capacity of the luminescent composite organohydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The SMGs owned self-recovery and fatigue resistance capacities, so that they could execute missions smoothly and consistently. Owing to the reversibility and constructability of the as-designed gels, the destroyed physical network was able to be reconstructed upon relaxing, rendering outstanding energy dissipation and self-recovery capacity to the composite organohydrogels. Fig. 6 a and 6b exhibited the self-recovery behavior of the CCP-OH gels upon a tensile strain of 100%. Compared with the original curve, the dissipated energy in the immediate curve maintained 53.1%, and the elastic modulus and maximum stress remained 33.1% and 84.1%, respectively. After resting for 15 min, the elastic modulus and maximum stress recovered 81.2% and 98.0%, respectively, approaching the original values. The rapid self-recovery ability empowered the CCP-OH gel with an eminent anti-fatigue property. Meanwhile, 10 and 100 times continuous cycles at strains of 100% and 50% were conducted to evaluate the stability of the CCP-OH gel. As shown in Fig. 6c and 6f, the overlapping stress–strain curve and similar loading–unloading curves further verified the preeminent anti-fatigue property of the CCP-OH gels. Otherwise, the loading–unloading curves of the hydrogels under various strains (Fig. 6d) also demonstrated the excellent mechanical performances of the CCP-OH gels. As the stretching strain increased from 10% to 200%, the network of the gels became more resilient. When one sample was cyclically stretched to different strains (Fig. 6e), the sample in the subsequent loading experience reduced stress before the previous strain, yet it reached similar stress at the same strain; further loading would lead to increased stress. This was probably induced by the partial self-recovery of the gels network, which further verified the preeminent anti-fatigue property and long-term durability of the CCP-OH gel.

In summary, it has fabricated novel SMGs with diverse fluorescent variation behavior during each stimulus (Fe3+, pH, and glucose)-based shape memory process. The SMGs which exhibited shape designing and fluorescence information encoding ability could be developed as information dual-encryption platform. It exhibited lots of outstanding characteristics, including superb mechanical performance (fracture stress up to 0.98 MPa, strain up to 448.6%), freezing tolerance (-25 °C), long-term solvent retention, self-recovery, and fatigue resistance capabilities. The shape memory behaviors were based on the reversible coordination interactions between Fe3+ and carboxylic acid groups of CMC, boric acid groups of CDs, and hydroxyl groups of PVA chains. The diversity fluorescence variation which corresponded to different stimuli (Fe3+, pH, and glucose) allowed one to monitor the shape variation performance of the gels in dark environments by fluorescence signals, and provided a feasible method for further study of the mechanism of the obtained shape memory hydrogels. Owing to the existence of DMSO, the organohydrogel maintained antibacterial properties and flexibility even at − 25 °C. The present strategy was expected to realize high through-put information encryption, protection, and display.

Yanqin Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yu Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xuehui Zhang: Investigation, Supervision, Resources. Fengbo Zhu: Investigation, Supervision. Yi-Xian Qin: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Weiyi Chen: Investigation, Supervision, Project administration. Qiang Zheng: Investigation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [No.51973183, 12172243], Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi [No.20210302123158], “Novel Polymer Functional Materials” Industry University Research Integrated Innovation Platform (DC2100000856) and the Fund for Shanxi “1331 project”.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.137930.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
